does: This paper investigates the effect of PCA whitening—a classical preprocessing technique that decorrelates input data—on sparse autoencoder training. The authors apply whitening to neural network activations before feeding them into SAEs and demonstrate improvements across multiple performance metrics. They conduct both theoretical analysis showing that whitening makes the optimization landscape more convex and empirical evaluation on SAEBench, comparing ReLU and Top-K SAE architectures across different model sizes and sparsity levels. Key findings include consistent improvements in interpretability metrics like sparse probing accuracy and feature disentanglement, though with some trade-off in reconstruction quality, suggesting whitening alters the fundamental optimization dynamics of SAE learning.
connects: This work builds on the long tradition of whitening and preprocessing techniques in classical sparse coding and signal processing, where decorrelating inputs has been a standard practice. It connects to recent progress in sparse autoencoders as interpretability tools for neural networks, extending methods developed by researchers studying feature extraction from activations. The paper engages with the classical sparse coding literature while applying these insights to modern deep learning interpretability, where SAEs have gained traction as alternatives to dictionary learning. It responds to ongoing challenges in SAE optimization by revisiting conventional preprocessing wisdom, implicitly connecting to broader questions about the relationship between data statistics and representation learning difficulty.
enables: This work suggests that interpretability and reconstruction need not align with conventional sparsity-fidelity trade-offs, opening new directions for designing SAE training procedures. It encourages reconsideration of classical signal processing techniques in modern neural network analysis and could inspire similar preprocessing interventions for other interpretability methods. The findings motivate further investigation into how data statistics shape feature learning, potentially leading to better optimization algorithms tailored for SAE training. Future work might explore whitening variants, its interaction with different sparsity constraints, and whether similar decorrelation strategies benefit other mechanistic interpretability approaches, expanding the toolkit for understanding and extracting interpretable representations from large neural networks.
